name: Stanton, Neville A.
recent_1: Shariati, A., Anvari, B., Kästle, J. L., Krol, J., Motaghedolhagh, K., Stanton, N. A., Reed, N., & Wurdemann, H. (2025). Analysing Variables Associated With Driver Reaction During the Transition From Automated to Manual Driving. IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, 26(10), 14536-14567. DOI: 10.1109/tits.2025.3594202
recent_2: Hulme, A., Stanton, N. A., Walker, G. H., Waterson, P., & Salmon, P. M. (2024). Testing the reliability of accident analysis methods: a comparison of AcciMap, STAMP-CAST and AcciNet. Ergonomics, 67(5), 695-715. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2023.2240048
recent_3: Salmon, P. M., McLean, S., Carden, T., King, B. J., Thompson, J., Baber, C., Stanton, N. A., & Read, G. J. M. (2024). When tomorrow comes: A prospective risk assessment of a future artificial general intelligence-based uncrewed combat aerial vehicle system. Applied Ergonomics, 117, 104245. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104245
recent_1_synopsis: A driving-simulator study examining how driver reaction during transitions from automated to manual control varies with operational and individual variables. The work continues Stanton's long line of takeover-of-control research, identifying factors that lengthen or shorten safe resumption of manual driving. Findings inform the design of transition warnings and handover protocols for highly automated vehicles.
recent_2_synopsis: An empirical reliability comparison of three systemic accident analysis methods (AcciMap, STAMP-CAST, and the newer AcciNet) applied by independent analysts to the same incident. The study quantifies inter-analyst agreement to test whether these widely used sociotechnical methods produce consistent findings. It contributes practical guidance on method selection and the methodological rigor of safety-science accident analysis.
recent_3_synopsis: A prospective, systems-level risk assessment of a hypothetical future artificial general intelligence (AGI)-controlled uncrewed combat aerial vehicle, using human factors risk-identification methods. It extends Stanton and colleagues' program on the safety and controllability risks of advanced AI in safety-critical and military sociotechnical systems. The paper argues that ergonomics methods can anticipate emergent hazards before such systems are deployed.
seminal_1: Eriksson, A., & Stanton, N. A. (2017). Takeover Time in Highly Automated Vehicles: Noncritical Transitions to and From Manual Control. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 59(4), 689-705. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816685832
seminal_2: Stanton, N. A., Stewart, R., Harris, D., Houghton, R. J., Baber, C., McMaster, R., Salmon, P., Hoyle, G., Walker, G., Young, M. S., Linsell, M., Dymott, R., & Green, D. (2006). Distributed situation awareness in dynamic systems: theoretical development and application of an ergonomics methodology. Ergonomics, 49(12-13), 1288-1311. DOI: 10.1080/00140130600612762
seminal_1_synopsis: A seminal driving-simulator study measuring how long drivers take to resume manual control after non-critical takeover requests in highly automated vehicles, establishing distributions of takeover times that became a benchmark for automated-driving handover research. Approximately 575 citations (Crossref, June 2026; ~1,600+ on Google Scholar). It is foundational because it provided some of the first systematic empirical evidence on transition-of-control timing, directly shaping HMI design guidelines and regulatory thinking for SAE Level 3 automation.
seminal_2_synopsis: A landmark theoretical and methodological paper that shifted situation awareness from an individual-cognition construct to a property distributed across human and non-human agents in a sociotechnical system, and introduced an ergonomics methodology to model it. Approximately 384 citations (Crossref, June 2026; well over 1,000 on Google Scholar). It is foundational because it launched the influential Distributed Situation Awareness (DSA) framework now widely applied across aviation, military command-and-control, energy, and transport domains.
expertise_overview: Stanton's research centers on modelling, predicting, analysing, and evaluating human performance in complex sociotechnical systems, and on designing the interaction between humans and technology. He is a leading developer and validator of human factors and ergonomics methods, including SHERPA (human error prediction), EAST (Event Analysis of Systemic Teamwork), cognitive work analysis, and the Distributed Situation Awareness framework. A major strand of his work addresses driving automation, vehicle interface design, takeover/transition of control, and road safety. He applies these methods across transport (automobiles, aircraft, ships, submarines), military command-and-control, healthcare, energy distribution, and control-room design. More recently his program has expanded to the human factors and safety risks of artificial intelligence and artificial general intelligence in safety-critical systems. His methodological toolkit spans simulator experiments, systems-analysis and accident-analysis methods, teamwork assessment, and sociotechnical systems theory.
notes: Identification confidence: very high. Confirmed as Neville A. Stanton, Emeritus Professor of Human Factors in Transport, University of Southampton (Transportation Research Group / hfesoton). Sources: Google Scholar profile (user=4miFHkcAAAAJ; ~51,800 total citations, h-index 113, accessed June 2026), University of Southampton faculty page, Wikipedia, ResearchGate, and Research.com. All four ground-truth Human Factors DOIs were independently re-verified via Crossref (200 + matching title/venue). Every recent and seminal DOI was verified via the Crossref API (api.crossref.org/works/<DOI>): IEEE TITS 2025 (10.1109/tits.2025.3594202), Ergonomics AcciMap 2024 (10.1080/00140139.2023.2240048), Applied Ergonomics AGI 2024 (10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104245), Takeover Time HF 2017 (10.1177/0018720816685832), and DSA Ergonomics 2006 (10.1080/00140130600612762). Stanton's co-authorship on all five was confirmed in the Crossref author lists. Citation counts cited above are Crossref is-referenced-by-count as of June 17 2026 (Takeover Time = 575; DSA = 384); Google Scholar totals are substantially higher (the Takeover Time and DSA papers each exceed 1,000+ on Scholar) but Scholar counts are not API-verifiable and are given only as approximate context. No DOIs were invented; no unverifiable DOIs were included. Note: an initially considered recent candidate ('Once a driver, always a driver', Applied Ergonomics 2024, 10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104366) was excluded because Crossref author metadata does not list Stanton.
